name: Mediterranean Diet
subtitle: Heart-healthy Mediterranean eating
category_tags: Health  |  Tags: Heart Healthy | Sustainable | Evidence Based
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (PREDIMED, Lyon Diet Heart Study)
about: A heart-healthy eating pattern inspired by the traditional cuisines of Greece, southern Italy, and coastal Spain. Built around olive oil as the primary fat, abundant vegetables and legumes, whole grains, moderate fish and poultry, and limited red meat.

Consistently ranked #1 overall diet by U.S. News & World Report for seven consecutive years. The PREDIMED trial (7,447 participants) demonstrated a 30% reduction in major cardiovascular events vs. low-fat control.
Associated with reduced risk of type 2 diabetes, cognitive decline, certain cancers, and depression. One of the most sustainable long-term patterns because nothing is forbidden.
In EatSense this is the flagship evidence-based program and the gold standard template. All other diets should match this level of UI completeness.
howItWorks: Base of every plate: non-starchy vegetables + whole grains (farro, bulgur, brown rice) + legumes (chickpeas, lentils, white beans)
Primary fat: extra-virgin olive oil for cooking and dressing (2-4 tbsp/day). Replace butter and seed oils entirely
Protein priority: fish/seafood 2-3x/week, poultry 2x/week, red meat max 1-2x/month in small portions
Daily: nuts/seeds (handful), fresh fruit as dessert, herbs and spices instead of salt
Moderate dairy: mainly yogurt and cheese (feta, pecorino), not processed dairy products
Optional: moderate red wine with meals (1 glass women, 1-2 men). Never required
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
5+ servings of vegetables and fruits today
Olive oil used as primary fat (no butter/seed oils)
Whole grains at every meal (not refined white flour)
Fish or legumes as main protein source
No ultra-processed food consumed today
Minimum 2L of water (not sugary drinks)
avoidList: Processed/cured meats (salami, hot dogs, bacon)
Refined grains (white bread, white pasta, pastries)
Added sugars and sugary beverages
Seed/vegetable oils (sunflower, canola, soybean)
Fast food and ultra-processed snacks
Excess red meat (max 1-2 small servings/month)
Scientific Sources & References
Estruch R et al. PREDIMED — Primary Prevention of CVD with Mediterranean Diet. NEJM 2018;378:e34
de Lorgeril M et al. Lyon Diet Heart Study. Circulation 1999
Sofi F et al. Mediterranean diet and health status: meta-analysis. BMJ 2008;337:a1344
WHO/FAO Nutrient Reference Values

name: DASH Diet
subtitle: Lower blood pressure naturally
category_tags: Health  |  Tags: Heart Healthy | Clinical | Evidence Based
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (NIH-funded RCTs)
about: Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension — a clinically proven eating plan developed by the U.S. National Institutes of Health. Designed specifically to lower blood pressure without medication, endorsed by the American Heart Association.

The original DASH trial showed blood pressure reductions within just 2 weeks. The DASH-Sodium trial demonstrated that combining DASH with sodium reduction (1,500mg/day) produced results comparable to first-line antihypertensive drugs.

Rich in potassium, magnesium, calcium, and fiber — minerals that regulate blood pressure. Emphasizes fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy while limiting sodium, saturated fat, and added sugars.
Beyond hypertension, DASH lowers LDL cholesterol, reduces risk of heart failure, kidney stones, and colorectal cancer.
howItWorks: Sodium target: 1,500-2,300mg/day max. Read every label — sodium hides in bread, sauces, canned foods
4-5 servings each of fruits AND vegetables daily (not combined — 4-5 of each)
2-3 servings low-fat dairy daily (milk, yogurt, cheese) for calcium and potassium
Whole grains 6-8 servings daily (oats, brown rice, whole wheat bread, quinoa)
Lean proteins: poultry, fish, beans. Limit red meat to 2 small servings/week
Nuts, seeds, legumes 4-5 times/week. Minimize added fats and sweets
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Sodium intake under 2,300mg (labels checked)
4-5 servings of fruits consumed
4-5 servings of vegetables consumed
Low-fat dairy serving included (yogurt, milk)
Whole grains at most meals
No added sugar or sugary drinks today
avoidList: High-sodium foods: canned soups, pickles, soy sauce, deli meats, frozen dinners
Cured/processed meats: bacon, salami, sausage, hot dogs
Salty snacks: chips, pretzels, salted nuts, crackers
Full-fat dairy products and butter
Sugary beverages: soda, sweetened tea, fruit juice
Excess alcohol (raises BP): max 1 drink/day women, 2 men
Scientific Sources & References
Sacks FM et al. DASH-Sodium. NEJM 2001;344:3-10
Appel LJ et al. Original DASH Trial. NEJM 1997;336:1117-24
AHA DASH Diet Recommendations (2024)
NIH NHLBI Guide to Lowering Blood Pressure with DASH

name: Flexitarian Diet
subtitle: Mostly plants, some meat
category_tags: Health  |  Tags: Sustainable | Flexible | Plant-Forward
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Moderate evidence
about: A flexible plant-based approach coined by dietitian Dawn Jackson Blatner. Eat mostly plants but don't eliminate meat entirely. The most popular semi-vegetarian pattern for people who want plant-based health benefits without rigid rules.

Research shows flexitarian diets are associated with lower body weight, improved metabolic markers, reduced diabetes risk, and lower environmental impact. The EAT-Lancet Commission recommends a similar pattern as optimal for both human and planetary health.
Structure: aim for 5 plant-based days and 2 days with meat/fish per week. On meat days, animal protein is a side dish (25% of plate), not the main event.
Ideal starter program for anyone curious about eating more plants. No forbidden foods, no complicated rules — emphasis on addition (more plants) rather than restriction.
howItWorks: 5 plant-based days, 2 meat/fish days per week. Start 3+4 if 5+2 feels ambitious
Plant-based protein sources: beans, lentils, tofu, tempeh, edamame, chickpeas, nuts, seeds
On meat days: animal protein as side dish (quarter plate), vegetables remain the star
Explore one new plant protein each week (try tempeh, seitan, or black bean burgers)
Whole grains daily: quinoa, brown rice, farro, whole wheat pasta, oats
Minimize processed food regardless of origin — vegan cookie is still a cookie
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Plant-based protein consumed today (beans, tofu, lentils)
5+ servings vegetables and fruits
Whole grains in at least one meal
New plant food tried this week
Meat-free meals today (aim for 2+)
Minimal processed food (plant-based junk counts too)
avoidList: Factory-farmed meat — choose quality when eating meat (organic, free-range)
Processed plant-based junk food (vegan hot dogs, fake cheese with additives)
Excessive dairy — treat as occasional, not daily
Ultra-processed snacks regardless of 'vegan' label
Relying on refined carbs to fill the gap when cutting meat
All-or-nothing thinking — partly plant-based day still counts
Scientific Sources & References
Blatner DJ. The Flexitarian Diet. McGraw-Hill, 2009
Derbyshire EJ. Flexitarian Diets and Health. Front Nutr 2017;3:55
Willett W et al. EAT-Lancet Commission. Lancet 2019;393:447-492
Sabate J & Soret S. Sustainability of plant-based diets. AJCN 2014

name: MIND Diet
subtitle: Brain-boosting nutrition
category_tags: Health  |  Tags: Brain Health | Anti-Aging | Evidence Based
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (Rush Memory & Aging Project)
about: Mediterranean-DASH Intervention for Neurodegenerative Delay. Developed by epidemiologist Martha Clare Morris at Rush University. Combines Mediterranean and DASH elements focusing specifically on brain-protective foods.

The Rush Memory and Aging Project found strict MIND adherence reduced Alzheimer's risk by up to 53%. Even moderate adherence reduced risk by ~35%. No other dietary pattern shows such strong neuroprotective effects.

Identifies 10 brain-healthy food groups to increase and 5 brain-unhealthy groups to minimize. Uniquely emphasizes berries (especially blueberries — shown to improve memory in clinical trials) and leafy greens (6+ servings/week).
What makes MIND unique vs. Mediterranean: specifically emphasizes berries and leafy greens while being less strict about overall fruit and fish intake.
howItWorks: Leafy greens at least 6 servings/week (spinach, kale, arugula, Swiss chard, mixed greens)
Berries at least 2x/week — especially blueberries and strawberries (only fruits specifically emphasized)
Nuts every day as snack — walnuts preferred (omega-3 ALA), almonds and pecans also good
Olive oil as primary cooking and dressing fat. Replace butter completely
Fish at least 1x/week (salmon, sardines, mackerel). Poultry 2+x/week
Beans and whole grains most days. Limit cheese to max 1x/week, red meat max 3x/week
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Leafy greens consumed today (spinach, kale, arugula)
Berries consumed this week (target: 2+ times)
Nuts eaten as daily snack (preferably walnuts)
Olive oil used as primary fat
Whole grains included in meals today
No fried food or pastries/sweets consumed
avoidList: Butter and stick margarine (limit <1 tbsp/day)
Cheese (limit <1 serving/week — stricter than Mediterranean)
Red meat (max 3 servings/week, preferably less)
Fried food and fast food
Pastries, sweets, candy, sugary beverages
Ultra-processed snack foods
Scientific Sources & References
Morris MC et al. MIND diet and Alzheimer's. Alzheimers Dement 2015;11(9):1007-14
Morris MC et al. MIND diet slows cognitive decline. Alzheimers Dement 2015;11(9):1015-22
Devore EE et al. Berries and cognitive decline. Ann Neurol 2012;72:135-143
Rush Memory and Aging Project (ongoing since 1997)

name: Nordic Diet
subtitle: Scandinavian-style healthy eating
category_tags: Health  |  Tags: Sustainable | Seasonal | Anti-Inflammatory
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Moderate evidence (SYSDIET, NORDIET)
about: Developed in 2004 by Danish chef Claus Meyer and University of Copenhagen researchers. Applies Mediterranean health principles using Scandinavian-native foods: fatty fish, root vegetables, rye, berries, canola oil.
The SYSDIET trial (166 participants) showed significant reduction in inflammation markers and improved cholesterol. The NORDIET trial confirmed improvements in blood lipids.
Core philosophy: eat locally, seasonally, sustainably. For Northern/Central European users, this is more practical and affordable than Mediterranean — all ingredients are locally available.
Emphasizes herring, mackerel, salmon, root vegetables, whole rye bread, wild berries, and canola oil as the primary fat.
howItWorks: Fatty fish 3-4x/week: salmon, herring, mackerel, sardines — richest omega-3 sources
Root vegetables daily: beetroot, carrots, parsnips, turnips, potatoes, celeriac
Whole grains daily — especially rye bread (rugbrod), oats, barley. Dark, dense breads preferred
Wild/foraged foods when available: berries, mushrooms, herbs. Frozen berries count year-round
Canola (rapeseed) oil as primary cooking fat — Nordic alternative to olive oil, also omega-3 rich
Game meats and free-range over conventional beef. Organ meats when possible
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Fatty fish consumed (salmon, herring, mackerel)
Root vegetables in at least one meal
Whole grain rye bread or oats consumed
Berries consumed (fresh or frozen)
Home-cooked meal with seasonal ingredients
Canola oil or Nordic fat used (not butter as main)
avoidList: Processed meats and fast food
White bread and refined grain products
Tropical fruits as daily staples (prioritize local berries/apples)
Excessive imported foods with high carbon footprint
Sugary drinks, candy, processed sweets
Non-canola seed oils (sunflower, corn, soybean)
Scientific Sources & References
Adamsson V et al. NORDIET. J Intern Med 2011;269:150-159
Uusitupa M et al. SYSDIET. J Intern Med 2013
Poulsen SK et al. Health effect of New Nordic Diet. Curr Opin Lipidol 2015
Meyer C. The Nordic Way. 2017

name: Paleo
subtitle: Whole foods, no grains or dairy
category_tags: Health  |  Tags: Ancestral | Whole Foods | Anti-Inflammatory
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Moderate  |  Evidence: Moderate evidence
about: Based on foods presumed available during the Paleolithic era (before agriculture). Core idea: our genes are mismatched with modern Western diet, and eating 'ancestrally' reduces inflammation and metabolic disease.

Multiple RCTs show paleo diets can improve waist circumference, triglycerides, blood pressure, and fasting blood sugar vs. conventional guidelines. Long-term adherence can be challenging due to eliminating grains, legumes, and dairy.
Emphasizes: meat, fish, eggs, vegetables, fruits, nuts, seeds, healthy oils. Eliminates: all grains, all legumes, all dairy, refined sugar, processed foods.
Quality matters: grass-fed meat, wild-caught fish, pasture-raised eggs preferred. One of the higher-cost dietary patterns.
howItWorks: Eat: meat, fish, seafood, eggs, vegetables, fruits, nuts, seeds, healthy oils (olive, avocado, coconut)
Eliminate completely: all grains, all legumes (beans, lentils, peanuts, soy), all dairy
No refined sugar, artificial sweeteners, or processed seed oils (canola, sunflower, soybean)
Prioritize quality: grass-fed beef, wild-caught fish, pasture-raised eggs, organic produce
No calorie counting — eat whole foods until satisfied. Protein and fat provide natural satiety
Cook at home — most restaurant/packaged foods contain non-paleo ingredients
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
No grains consumed (no bread, rice, oats, pasta, corn)
No dairy consumed (no milk, cheese, yogurt, butter)
No legumes consumed (no beans, lentils, peanuts, soy)
Quality protein at every meal
Vegetables at every meal (non-starchy preferred)
Adequate healthy fats from whole sources (avocado, nuts, olive oil)
avoidList: All grains: wheat, rice, oats, barley, corn, spelt, kamut (including 'whole grain')
All legumes: beans, lentils, chickpeas, peanuts, soy products
All dairy: milk, cheese, yogurt, butter, cream, ice cream
Refined sugar, HFCS, artificial sweeteners
Processed seed oils: canola, sunflower, safflower, corn, soybean
All processed/packaged foods, fast food, alcohol (strict paleo)
Scientific Sources & References
Lindeberg S et al. Palaeolithic diet and glucose tolerance. Diabetologia 2007
Frassetto LA et al. Metabolic improvements from paleolithic diet. Eur J Clin Nutr 2009
Manheimer EW et al. Paleolithic nutrition meta-analysis. AJCN 2015
Cordain L. The Paleo Diet. Wiley, 2002

name: Clean Eating (Simple)
subtitle: Whole foods, home cooking
category_tags: Health  |  Tags: Beginner Friendly | Whole Foods | Sustainable
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: General consensus (NOVA classification)
about: The simplest healthy eating approach: choose whole, minimally processed foods and cook at home. No macros, no elimination, no calorie counting. If the ingredient list is short and recognizable, it's fine.
Aligns with the NOVA classification system: eat mostly Group 1 (unprocessed/minimally processed) and Group 2 (culinary ingredients like olive oil), minimize Group 4 (ultra-processed).

Studies consistently show ultra-processed food consumption correlates with obesity, CVD, diabetes, depression, and all-cause mortality. Simply reducing UPF intake improves health markers without other changes.
Ideal for absolute beginners or anyone overwhelmed by complex dietary rules. Emphasis on food quality and home cooking rather than restricting nutrients or food groups.
howItWorks: Choose foods with 5 or fewer recognizable ingredients (or no ingredient list — whole foods)
The grandmother test: if a 1950s grandmother wouldn't recognize it as food, skip it
Cook at least one meal at home every single day
Read labels on everything packaged: avoid artificial colors, preservatives, unpronounceable ingredients
Eat the rainbow — variety of colorful produce daily (different colors = different nutrients)
Water, tea, coffee as primary drinks. Eliminate sugary drinks and diet sodas
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Home-cooked meal today (at least one)
Labels checked on all packaged food
Avoided foods with 5+ ingredients or artificial additives
Colorful plate achieved (3+ colors of produce)
8 glasses of water consumed
No fast food or ultra-processed meals today
avoidList: Ultra-processed foods (NOVA Group 4): soft drinks, packaged snacks, instant noodles, frozen meals
Artificial sweeteners, colors, preservatives (aspartame, Red 40, BHT)
Foods with ingredient lists >5 items or unrecognizable names
Fast food chains
Refined white flour: white bread, pastries, commercial baked goods
Sugary beverages including juice from concentrate and diet soda
Scientific Sources & References
Monteiro CA et al. Ultra-processed foods: what they are. Public Health Nutr 2019
Srour B et al. Ultra-processed food and CVD risk. BMJ 2019;365:l1451
Hall KD et al. Ultra-Processed Diets and Weight Gain (NIH RCT). Cell Metab 2019
NOVA Food Classification System

name: Balanced Plate Method
subtitle: Simple visual portion guide
category_tags: Popular  |  Tags: Beginner Friendly | No Counting | Visual
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (Harvard Healthy Eating Plate)
about: The simplest evidence-based approach: divide your plate visually. Half vegetables/fruits, quarter protein, quarter whole grains. No counting, no scales — just your eyes and a plate.
Developed by Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health as a response to USDA MyPlate. Harvard felt MyPlate was too influenced by the food industry, especially regarding dairy requirements.

Naturally controls portions and calories without tracking. When half your plate is vegetables, you automatically eat fewer calories while getting more fiber, vitamins, and minerals. It's a behavioral strategy, not just nutritional.
EatSense's recommended starting point for new users. Many find this method alone sufficient for their goals.
howItWorks: Half plate (50%): non-starchy vegetables and fruits — salad, broccoli, peppers, tomatoes, leafy greens
Quarter plate (25%): lean protein — chicken, fish, tofu, eggs, beans, lentils (palm-sized portion)
Quarter plate (25%): whole grains or starchy vegetables — brown rice, quinoa, sweet potato, oats
Add thumb-sized healthy fat: olive oil drizzle, quarter avocado, small handful of nuts
Fruit as dessert or part of veggie/fruit half. Whole fruit over juice
Water, tea, or coffee. Skip sugary drinks. Apply to every meal including breakfast
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Half plate vegetables/fruits at every meal
Quarter plate lean protein at every meal
Quarter plate whole grains at every meal
Healthy fat included (olive oil, avocado, or nuts)
Water as main drink (no sugary beverages)
Portion sizes respected (no oversized plates or seconds)
avoidList: Oversized portions (one even layer, not piled high)
Skipping the vegetable half (biggest mistake people make)
Calorie-dense sauces/dressings (measure them)
Mindless snacking between meals
Drinking calories: sugary drinks, fancy coffees, juice
Treating starchy veg (potatoes, corn) as 'vegetables' — grain quarter
Scientific Sources & References
Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — Healthy Eating Plate (2011)
USDA MyPlate Guidelines (comparison)
Rolls BJ. Volumetrics Eating Plan. HarperCollins, 2005
Romagnolo DF & Selmin OI. Mediterranean Diet and Chronic Disease. Nutr Today 2017

name: Keto Diet
subtitle: Very low-carb, high-fat
category_tags: Medical  |  Tags: Ketosis | Therapeutic | Fat Adapted
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Hard  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (epilepsy; moderate for weight loss)
about: Very low-carbohydrate, high-fat diet shifting primary fuel from glucose to ketone bodies. Nutritional ketosis occurs at blood ketones 0.5-3.0 mmol/L, requiring strict carb restriction: 20-50g net carbs/day.

Originally developed in the 1920s at Mayo Clinic for epilepsy treatment. Now popular for weight loss and metabolic health. Clinical evidence supports improvements in epilepsy, short-term weight loss, and type 2 diabetes markers.

Long-term adherence is challenging — most studies show high dropout rates beyond 12 months. The 'keto flu' (fatigue, headache, irritability) is common during adaptation (weeks 1-2). Electrolyte supplementation is essential.
Medical considerations: people with kidney disease, liver disease, pancreatitis, or type 1 diabetes must consult their doctor before starting.
howItWorks: Limit net carbs (total - fiber) to 20-50g/day. Track carefully, especially first 2 weeks
70-80% of daily calories from fat: avocado, olive oil, coconut oil, butter, nuts, cheese, fatty fish/meat
Moderate protein: 20-25% calories (excessive protein → glucose via gluconeogenesis)
Eliminate: all sugar, bread, pasta, rice, potatoes, most fruits. Berries in small amounts OK
Supplement electrolytes: 5-7g sodium/day, magnesium and potassium to prevent keto flu
Drink 3+ liters water daily — ketosis has diuretic effect, dehydration is #1 side effect
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Net carbs under 50g today (weighed and tracked)
Fat was primary energy source (70-80% calories)
Electrolytes supplemented (sodium, magnesium, potassium)
Adequate protein (not too much, not too little)
No sugar in any form consumed
Hydration: 3+ liters water
avoidList: All sugar: table sugar, honey, maple, agave, candy (except 90%+ dark chocolate sparingly)
All grains: bread, pasta, rice, cereal, oats, corn, flour
Starchy vegetables: potatoes, sweet potatoes, corn, peas, butternut squash
Most fruits: bananas, apples, oranges, grapes, mango (small berries OK)
Legumes: beans, lentils, chickpeas, peanuts (PB sparingly)
Beer, cocktails, sweet wine. Dry wine/spirits in very limited amounts
Scientific Sources & References
Paoli A et al. Therapeutic uses of ketogenic diets. Eur J Clin Nutr 2013
Volek JS & Phinney SD. Art and Science of Low Carb Living. 2011
Neal EG et al. Ketogenic diet for childhood epilepsy RCT. Lancet Neurol 2008
Westman EC et al. Low-carb nutrition and metabolism. AJCN 2007

name: Low-FODMAP (Gut)
subtitle: Gut-healing diet in phases
category_tags: Medical  |  Tags: IBS Relief | Gut Health | Therapeutic
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 42 days  |  Level: Hard  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (Monash University)
about: Three-phase therapeutic elimination diet from Monash University for IBS management. FODMAP = Fermentable Oligosaccharides, Disaccharides, Monosaccharides And Polyols — poorly absorbed short-chain carbohydrates.

In clinical trials, 50-80% of IBS patients experience significant symptom improvement. Recommended as first-line dietary therapy by the British Dietetic Association and American Gastroenterological Association.
Phase 1: Elimination (2-6 weeks) removes all high-FODMAP foods. Phase 2: Reintroduction (6-8 weeks) tests one group at a time. Phase 3: Personalization (ongoing) avoids only identified triggers.
Critical: NOT a permanent diet. Elimination >6 weeks harms gut microbiome diversity. It's a diagnostic tool, not a lifestyle. Work with a FODMAP-trained dietitian for best results.
howItWorks: Phase 1 (Wk 1-3): strictly avoid all high-FODMAP foods. Use Monash FODMAP app for safe portions
Phase 2 (Wk 4-6): test one FODMAP subgroup every 3 days (fructose, lactose, fructans, GOS, polyols)
Phase 3 (Wk 7+): personalized long-term diet avoiding only YOUR specific triggers
Keep detailed food + symptom diary every day — rate bloating, pain, gas, bowel habits 1-10
Eat enough fiber from safe sources (oats, rice, potatoes, banana, kiwi) to avoid constipation
Strongly recommended: work with FODMAP-trained dietitian. Self-guiding risks unnecessary restriction
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
All high-FODMAP foods avoided today (Phase 1)
Food diary updated with all meals and portions
Symptom diary updated (bloating, pain, gas 1-10)
Adequate fiber from safe sources (oats, rice, banana)
Stress management practiced (stress worsens IBS)
Adequate hydration (2L+ water)
avoidList: High-fructose: apples, pears, watermelon, mango, honey, HFCS, agave
Lactose: regular milk, soft cheese, yogurt, ice cream (lactose-free OK)
Fructans: ONION, GARLIC (biggest triggers!), wheat, rye, artichoke
GOS: beans, lentils, chickpeas, soy milk, cashews, pistachios
Polyols: mushrooms, cauliflower, stone fruits, avocado (large), sugar-free gum
Note: garlic-INFUSED oil IS safe (fructans not oil-soluble). Garlic pieces are NOT safe
Scientific Sources & References
Halmos EP et al. Low FODMAP and IBS. Gastroenterology 2014;146:67-75
Monash University FODMAP Diet (gold standard reference & app)
Staudacher HM et al. Low FODMAP mechanisms. Gut 2017;66:1517-1527
British Dietetic Association Low FODMAP Systematic Review, 2016

name: Gluten-Free
subtitle: For celiac disease or gluten sensitivity
category_tags: Medical  |  Tags: Celiac Safe | Allergen-Free | Therapeutic
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Moderate  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (essential for celiac)
about: Completely eliminates gluten (protein in wheat, barley, rye). Medically essential for celiac disease (~1% of population), wheat allergy, and non-celiac gluten sensitivity.
For celiac patients, even trace amounts (10-50mg — a single breadcrumb) trigger autoimmune damage to intestinal villi, causing malabsorption and systemic symptoms.
Gluten hides everywhere: soy sauce (contains wheat), seasonings, salad dressings, medications, supplements, even cosmetics. Cross-contamination in shared kitchens is a major risk.
Focus on naturally GF whole foods (meat, fish, eggs, vegetables, fruit, nuts, rice, quinoa, buckwheat) rather than processed GF substitutes which are often high in sugar and refined starch.
howItWorks: Eliminate completely: wheat (all forms), barley, rye, spelt, kamut, triticale
Oats: ONLY certified 'Gluten-Free' oats — regular oats are cross-contaminated
Read EVERY label: wheat, barley, rye, malt, brewer's yeast, modified food starch
Cross-contamination prevention: separate cutting boards, toaster, colander in shared kitchens
Safe grains: rice, quinoa, buckwheat, millet, corn, potato, sweet potato, tapioca, amaranth, teff
Prioritize naturally GF whole foods over processed GF products
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
No gluten consumed (wheat, barley, rye checked)
Labels checked on ALL packaged food
Cross-contamination prevented (separate utensils)
Naturally GF whole foods prioritized over processed GF products
Adequate fiber intake (GF diets can be low in fiber)
Iron and B-vitamin intake monitored (common celiac deficiencies)
avoidList: Wheat: bread, pasta, flour, couscous, bulgur, seitan (pure gluten!)
Barley: malt, malt vinegar, malt flavoring, many beers
Rye: rye bread, rye flour, some whiskeys
Regular oats (cross-contaminated) — only certified GF oats
Hidden: regular soy sauce, many dressings, gravies with flour, some medications
Cross-contaminated: shared fryers, shared toasters, bulk bins
Scientific Sources & References
Fasano A & Catassi C. Celiac Disease. NEJM 2012;367:2419-2426
Celiac Disease Foundation Guidelines (celiac.org)
Biesiekierski JR et al. Gluten and GI symptoms in non-celiac. AJCN 2011
Codex Alimentarius GF Standard (<20 ppm)

name: Low-Carb Diet
subtitle: Reduced carbs for weight management
category_tags: Weight Loss  |  Tags: Fat Loss | Blood Sugar Control | Satiety
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Moderate  |  Evidence: Strong evidence
about: Moderate carb restriction (50-130g/day) between standard diet (~250-300g) and strict keto (<50g). Reduces blood sugar spikes, improves insulin sensitivity, promotes fat burning while being more sustainable than very low-carb.
The A TO Z Study (JAMA 2007) and DIRECT trial (NEJM 2008) showed low-carb produces equal or greater weight loss vs. low-fat, with better triglycerides and HDL cholesterol.
Particularly effective for insulin resistance, prediabetes, PCOS, metabolic syndrome. For insulin-sensitive, highly active individuals, the advantage over balanced diets may be smaller.
Unlike keto, does not require ketosis or tracking every gram. Fruits, some whole grains, and starchy vegetables are allowed. Practical for restaurants and long-term adherence.
howItWorks: Limit total carbs to 50-130g/day (start 100g, adjust based on energy and results)
Protein at every meal: palm-sized portion. Protein improves satiety and preserves muscle
Non-starchy vegetables unlimited: leafy greens, broccoli, cauliflower, peppers, zucchini
Complex carbs when you eat them: sweet potato > bread, berries > bananas, quinoa > white rice
Healthy fats for satisfaction: olive oil, avocado, nuts. Don't go low-fat AND low-carb
Front-load carbs earlier in day or around exercise. Lower-carb dinner for sleep and blood sugar
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Total carbs under 130g today
Protein at every meal (palm-sized minimum)
Non-starchy vegetables at 3+ meals
No refined carbs (white bread, pastries, white rice)
Complex carbs chosen when carbs were eaten
Healthy fats included for satisfaction
avoidList: Bread, bagels, tortillas, baked goods
Pasta (use zucchini noodles or shirataki)
White rice and high-starch foods in large portions
Sugary foods: candy, cookies, cake, dried fruit
Fruit juice, soda, sweetened coffee, sports drinks
Cereal and granola (typically 40-60g carbs/serving)
Scientific Sources & References
Gardner CD et al. A TO Z Weight Loss Study. JAMA 2007;297:969-77
Shai I et al. DIRECT trial. NEJM 2008;359:229-41
Brehm BJ et al. Low-carb diet RCT. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2003
Westman EC et al. Low-carb nutrition. AJCN 2007;86:276-84

name: Intermittent Fasting 16:8
subtitle: 16h fast, 8h eating window
category_tags: Weight Loss  |  Tags: Time-Restricted | Autophagy | Fat Burning
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Moderate  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (NEJM 2019)
about: The most popular intermittent fasting pattern: eat within 8 hours, fast 16 (including sleep). Most skip breakfast, eating 12pm-8pm.

NEJM review (de Cabo & Mattson 2019) confirms IF improves insulin sensitivity, reduces inflammation, promotes autophagy, and may improve cardiovascular and brain health — partly independent of weight loss.
Naturally reduces caloric intake 10-20% without conscious restriction. Combined with metabolic fasting benefits, creates favorable fat loss conditions.
During fast: water, black coffee (no sugar/milk), plain tea. Artificial sweeteners are debated — some may trigger insulin response.
howItWorks: Choose 8-hour window: most popular 12pm-8pm. Adjust to your schedule
16h fast: only water, black coffee, plain herbal tea. Zero calories
Break fast with protein + healthy fats first (eggs, salmon, avocado) — NOT sugar/carbs
Eat 2-3 quality meals within window. No calorie restriction needed but eat whole foods
Last meal 2-3 hours before bed for sleep quality
Stay consistent — adaptation takes 1-2 weeks. Morning hunger disappears
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
16-hour fast maintained (no calories consumed)
No calories during fast (no milk in coffee, no sugar)
First meal included protein + healthy fats
Quality whole foods in eating window
Adequate water during fast (2L+ minimum)
Last meal finished 2-3h before bed
avoidList: Any calories during fast — even splash of milk breaks it
Breaking fast with sugar/simple carbs (pastries, juice, cereal)
Late-night eating past window
Overcompensating with excess food in window
Sugary drinks and alcohol in window
Chewing gum with sugar during fast
Scientific Sources & References
de Cabo R & Mattson MP. Intermittent Fasting. NEJM 2019;381:2541-2551
Sutton EF et al. Early TRF and insulin. Cell Metab 2018;27:1212-1221
Tinsley GM & La Bounty PM. IF and body composition. JISSN 2015;12:1-8
Anton SD et al. Metabolic switching. Obesity 2018;26:254-268

name: 14:10 Eating Window
subtitle: Gentle fasting: 14h fast, 10h window
category_tags: Weight Loss  |  Tags: Beginner Fasting | Gentle | Sustainable
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Moderate evidence (Wilkinson 2020)
about: Gentler time-restricted eating: 14h overnight fast, 10h window. Many people are already close to this naturally (8am-6pm). Ideal IF starting point.

Wilkinson et al. (Cell Metab 2020) found 10-hour eating window improved metabolic markers: reduced body weight, blood pressure, LDL, HbA1c in metabolic syndrome patients — without changing WHAT they ate.
Benefit comes from extending overnight fast enough to improve insulin sensitivity and allow more 'repair mode' time. Most fasting happens during sleep, making this extremely practical.
Designed as stepping stone — users comfortable after 2-3 weeks can optionally progress to 16:8. But 14:10 alone provides meaningful benefits.
howItWorks: Choose 10-hour window: e.g., 8am-6pm or 9am-7pm
14h fast (mostly overnight): water, herbal tea, black coffee only
Eat 3 balanced meals within window — no need to skip breakfast
Food quality focus: works even without changing what you eat, but whole foods amplify results
Consistent timing daily to align with circadian rhythm
After 2-3 weeks, optionally narrow to 12h, then 10h, then 8h window
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Eating window respected (all food within 10 hours)
No late-night eating after window
3 balanced meals within window
Adequate hydration (2L+ water)
No calorie drinks outside window
Consistent meal timing maintained
avoidList: Eating outside 10-hour window (especially late-night snacking)
Calorie drinks after window closes
Irregular schedules (shifting window daily reduces benefit)
Skipping meals to 'save calories'
Excessive caffeine late in day (coffee before 2pm)
Compensatory overeating ('window closing soon' panic)
Scientific Sources & References
Wilkinson MJ et al. Ten-Hour TRE. Cell Metab 2020;31:92-104
Longo VD & Panda S. Fasting, Circadian Rhythms, TRF. Cell Metab 2016
Chaix A et al. TRF as preventative intervention. Cell Metab 2014
Panda S. The Circadian Code. Rodale Books, 2018

name: Whole30 (Inspired)
subtitle: 30-day elimination reset
category_tags: Weight Loss  |  Tags: Elimination | Reset | Discipline
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Hard  |  Evidence: Anecdotal / elimination diet evidence
about: Strict 30-day elimination removing sugar, alcohol, grains, legumes, soy, dairy, and additives. Created by Melissa Hartwig Urban to reset eating habits and identify food sensitivities.
Not primarily about weight loss — designed to break sugar addiction, eliminate mindless snacking, understand food triggers, and discover how foods make you feel.
Rules are absolute: any restricted food requires restarting from Day 1. 'No recreating junk food with approved ingredients' rule prevents psychological loopholes (no paleo pancakes).
After Day 30: structured reintroduction adds back one food group every 2-3 days while monitoring body response. This phase is as important as elimination.
howItWorks: No sugar of any kind: table sugar, honey, maple, stevia, agave, artificial sweeteners — all eliminated
No alcohol — not even for cooking (wine in sauce, beer batter)
No grains: wheat, rice, oats, corn, quinoa, buckwheat — all eliminated
No legumes: beans, lentils, chickpeas, peanuts, soy (tofu, tempeh, soy sauce)
No dairy: milk, cheese, yogurt, butter, cream. Clarified butter (ghee) IS allowed
No recreating banned foods with approved ingredients. After Day 30: structured reintroduction
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
No sugar today (including honey, maple, stevia, artificial)
No alcohol (including in cooking)
No grains (wheat, rice, oats, corn, quinoa)
No dairy or soy
No legumes (beans, lentils, peanuts)
All labels checked (sugar and soy hide everywhere)
avoidList: ALL sugar and sweeteners: table sugar, honey, maple, stevia, monk fruit, aspartame
ALL alcohol: wine, beer, spirits, cooking wine, vanilla extract with alcohol
ALL grains: wheat, rice, oats, corn, quinoa, buckwheat, millet
ALL dairy: milk, cheese, yogurt, butter (ghee OK), cream, whey protein
ALL legumes: beans, chickpeas, lentils, peanuts, PB, soy in all forms
Carrageenan, MSG, sulfites. No 'paleo treats' recreating banned foods
Scientific Sources & References
Hartwig Urban M. The Whole30. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2015
Whole30.com Official Program Rules
Elimination diet principles: Brostoff J & Gamlin L. Food Allergies and Intolerance. 2000
Note: Whole30 is trademarked. EatSense implementation is 'inspired by' the methodology

name: High-Protein Cut
subtitle: High protein, fat loss, muscle preservation
category_tags: Performance  |  Tags: Muscle Preservation | Fat Loss | Sports
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Moderate  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (Phillips, Helms, JISSN)
about: Structured caloric deficit prioritizing protein (1.6-2.2g/kg) to preserve muscle during fat loss. The evidence-based approach for body recomposition used by physique athletes.
Phillips et al. (2016) and Helms et al. (2014) established optimal protein targets during deficit. Higher protein increases satiety, has 25-30% thermic effect, and reduces muscle loss risk.
Moderate deficit of 300-500 kcal below TDEE. Aggressive deficits (>750 kcal) increase muscle loss regardless of protein. Target 0.5-1.0% BW loss per week.
REQUIRES resistance training. Deficit + high protein WITHOUT lifting still causes significant muscle loss. All three combined is what makes cuts effective.
howItWorks: Calculate TDEE, subtract 300-500 calories for daily target
Protein: 1.6-2.2g/kg bodyweight daily. For 75kg = 120-165g protein/day
Distribute protein across 4-5 meals (25-40g each) for optimal muscle protein synthesis
Largest carb intake around workouts: pre-workout (2h before) and post-workout (within 1h)
Don't cut fat below 20% total calories — hormones require adequate fat
Weekly refeed at maintenance calories (higher carbs) to prevent metabolic adaptation
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Protein target hit (1.8g+/kg bodyweight)
Caloric deficit maintained (300-500 below TDEE)
4+ protein meals consumed (evenly distributed)
Pre/post workout nutrition completed
Adequate sleep (7+ hours — critical during deficit)
Resistance training session completed
avoidList: Alcohol — impairs muscle protein synthesis by up to 37%
Calorie-dense sauces (200-500 hidden calories)
Skipping meals — uneven protein distribution increases muscle breakdown
Aggressive deficit >500 calories (accelerates muscle loss)
Low-protein meals (<20g) — wasted muscle opportunity
Relying on cardio alone without resistance training
Scientific Sources & References
Phillips SM et al. Protein and muscle retention during deficit. AJCN 2016
Helms ER et al. Protein during caloric restriction. JISSN 2014;11:20
Jager R et al. ISSN Position Stand: protein and exercise. JISSN 2017;14:20
Mero AA. Protein and muscle. J Sports Sci 1999

name: Flexible Dieting (IIFYM)
subtitle: Hit your macros, flexible food choices
category_tags: Performance  |  Tags: Macro Counting | Flexible | Sports
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 30 days  |  Level: Moderate  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (Aragon & Schoenfeld, JISSN)
about: If It Fits Your Macros — no food is forbidden if you hit daily protein, carb, and fat targets. Body doesn't distinguish 50g carbs from sweet potato vs. cookie at the macro level.
Aragon & Schoenfeld (2014) confirmed no significant body composition difference between 'clean' and 'flexible' dieting when calories and macros are matched.
80/20 approach: 80% whole foods (micronutrients, fiber, health), 20% whatever you want (enjoyment, sustainability). Prevents binge-restrict cycle.
Requires accurate tracking — weigh food on kitchen scale and log everything. Discipline is in tracking, not food selection.
howItWorks: Calculate TDEE, set target based on goal (deficit/surplus/maintenance)
Set protein first: 1.6-2.2g/kg athletes, 1.2-1.6g/kg general fitness. Non-negotiable
Set fat minimum: 20-30% total calories (hormones, cell function)
Fill remaining calories with carbs (fuel training performance)
Track everything in EatSense: weigh food, scan barcodes, log every bite
80/20 rule: 80% whole foods, 20% whatever fits your macros
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
All macros hit within 5g of target
Every food weighed and logged accurately
80/20 rule followed (majority whole foods)
Protein target met (highest priority)
Training performance maintained/improved
Weekly body weight and measurements tracked
avoidList: Guessing portions — weigh everything (first 4-8 weeks minimum)
Ignoring fiber (aim 25g+/day from whole foods)
100% junk food even if it 'fits' — micronutrients matter
Eating-back exercise calories (track separately)
Comparing your macros to others (individual needs vary)
Obsessing over daily fluctuations — track weekly trends
Scientific Sources & References
Aragon AA & Schoenfeld BJ. Macros and body composition. JISSN 2014;11:36
Helms ER et al. Natural bodybuilding recommendations. JISSN 2014;11:20
Stewart TM et al. Rigid vs. flexible dieting. Appetite 2002;38:39-44
Linardon J. Flexible control and disordered eating. Eat Behav 2017

name: Lean Bulk
subtitle: Build muscle without the fat
category_tags: Performance  |  Tags: Muscle Gain | Bulking | Performance
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 14 days  |  Level: Moderate  |  Evidence: Strong evidence (Slater, Iraki, JISSN)
about: Strategic caloric surplus (200-350 above TDEE) to maximize muscle gain while minimizing fat. Unlike dirty bulking, lean bulk optimizes the muscle-to-fat ratio.
Slater et al. (2019): weight gain should not exceed 0.25-0.5% BW/week for trained individuals. Faster gain = more fat, not more muscle. For 75kg person: 0.2-0.4kg/week.
Progressive overload is non-negotiable: without increasing training volume/intensity, extra calories become fat. Surplus provides energy; training provides stimulus.
14-day program — lean bulking in short structured phases with regular assessment. After 14 days: gaining too fast? Reduce surplus. Too slow? Increase.
howItWorks: TDEE + 200-350 calories (not more). More surplus = more fat, not more muscle
Protein: 1.6-2.2g/kg bodyweight daily — same target as cutting
Carbs are training fuel: fill most surplus with carbs, especially around workouts
Largest meals pre-workout (2-3h before) and post-workout (within 1h)
Progressive overload mandatory: track every workout, add reps or weight weekly
Weigh daily (morning, fasted), track weekly average. >0.5% BW/week → reduce surplus
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Protein target hit (1.8g+/kg)
Caloric surplus maintained (200-350 above TDEE)
Pre-workout meal eaten (2-3h before)
Post-workout nutrition consumed (protein + carbs within 1h)
Resistance training with progressive overload
Weekly average weight tracked (daily weigh-ins)
avoidList: Dirty bulking: eating everything leads to excess fat requiring longer cut
Skipping training sessions: without stimulus, surplus = fat
Excessive surplus (>500 above TDEE): max muscle growth rate is ~0.5-1kg/month
Alcohol: impairs muscle protein synthesis by up to 37%
Ignoring the scale: >0.5% BW/week = reduce intake
Cutting all cardio: 2-3 light sessions/week supports heart health and nutrient partitioning
Scientific Sources & References
Slater GJ et al. Energy surplus and hypertrophy. J Sports Sci 2019;37:2560-2570
Iraki J et al. Off-season bodybuilding nutrition. JISSN 2019;16:1-14
Schoenfeld BJ et al. Protein per meal for muscle. JISSN 2018;15:10
Ribeiro AS et al. Resistance training and health markers. Age 2015
weeklyShoppingList: BUG: Tag 'diets.tags.bulk' shows raw i18n key. Daily Tracker has only 1 item. Fix both.

name: Summer Fresh
subtitle: Fresh summer eating plan
category_tags: Seasonal  |  Tags: Seasonal | Hydrating | Light
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 14 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Seasonal nutrition principles
about: Light, hydrating meals for hot summer. Built around peak-season produce: berries, stone fruits, tomatoes, zucchini, watermelon, cucumbers, peppers, herbs.
Summer eating naturally shifts to raw/cold preparations: salads, smoothie bowls, gazpacho, ceviche, grilled foods. These preserve nutrients and keep the kitchen cool.
Hydration is core focus. Summer fluid needs increase 50-100%. High-water-content foods (watermelon 92% water, cucumber 96%) supplement drinking water.
Calories intentionally lighter — heat reduces appetite and body needs fewer calories for temperature regulation. Increase portions if very active.
howItWorks: Hydrating foods every meal: cucumber, watermelon, tomatoes, zucchini, berries, citrus
Lighter portions — respect reduced summer appetite. 3 light meals + snacks
Raw/cold preparations: salads, smoothie bowls, gazpacho, cold soups, poke
Grill instead of oven/stove — flavor without heating kitchen
Seasonal berries and stone fruits daily at peak nutrition
Hydration: 3+ liters water/day. Add electrolytes if sweating heavily
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
3+ liters water consumed
Seasonal summer fruit in meals
Light, hydrating meal consumed
Raw or lightly cooked vegetables included
No heavy fried or cream-based dishes
Outdoor activity or movement completed
avoidList: Heavy cream sauces and rich dishes
Large hot meals (stews, casseroles, heavy baked pasta)
Dehydrating alcohol (alternate 1:1 with water if drinking)
Excessive caffeine (increases dehydration in heat)
Sugary frozen drinks (frappuccinos, slushies, chain smoothies)
Ignoring dehydration signs: dark urine, headache, fatigue
Scientific Sources & References
USDA Seasonal Produce Guide — Summer
Popkin BM et al. Water, hydration, health. Nutr Rev 2010
EFSA Dietary Reference Values for Water (2010)
Seasonal Mediterranean cuisine patterns

name: Winter Warmth
subtitle: Warming winter eating plan
category_tags: Seasonal  |  Tags: Seasonal | Comfort | Immune Support
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 21 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Seasonal & immune support principles
about: Nourishing meals for cold months: root vegetables, squash, citrus, dark leafy greens, warming spices. Soups, stews, roasted dishes that fuel the body through shorter, darker days.
Immune support is key: vitamin C (citrus, bell peppers, kiwi), zinc (meat, pumpkin seeds), vitamin D (fatty fish, eggs — critical in low-sunlight months), probiotics (fermented foods).
Calories slightly higher than summer — body expends more energy maintaining core temperature. Hearty winter meals provide the fuel needed.
Vitamin D supplementation recommended above 40th latitude (most of Europe) November through March.
howItWorks: Warming spices daily: ginger, turmeric, cinnamon, cayenne, black pepper, cumin, cardamom
Root vegetables as staples: sweet potato, parsnips, carrots, beets, turnips, celeriac
Soups and stews as main meals 3-4x/week — nutrient-dense, warming, easy to batch cook
Citrus daily for vitamin C: oranges, grapefruit, clementines, lemons
Warm breakfasts every morning: porridge, warm eggs, sauteed greens
Vitamin D: fatty fish 2-3x/week, eggs, and consider 1000-2000 IU supplement
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Warming spices used in at least one meal
Root vegetables in at least one meal
Citrus fruit consumed for vitamin C
Soup, stew, or warm dish as main meal
Vitamin D source included (fish, eggs, or supplement)
Warm breakfast eaten (not cold cereal)
avoidList: Cold salads as main meals (eat as sides; warm dishes more satiating)
Excessive holiday sweets (enjoy occasionally, not daily)
Skipping vitamin D (insufficient sunlight Nov-March in Northern Europe)
Dehydration (cold reduces thirst but fluid needs remain — drink warm water/tea)
High-calorie but low-nutrient comfort food (white bread, pastries, fried)
Ignoring SAD: combine nutrition with light therapy, exercise, social connection
Scientific Sources & References
NIH Vitamin D Fact Sheet for Health Professionals (2023)
Martineau AR et al. Vitamin D and respiratory infections. BMJ 2017
USDA Seasonal Produce Guide — Winter
EFSA Vitamin D Tolerable Upper Intake (2012)

name: Autumn Harvest
subtitle: Seasonal autumn eating
category_tags: Seasonal  |  Tags: Seasonal | Harvest | Transitional
duration_level_evidence: Duration: 21 days  |  Level: Easy  |  Evidence: Seasonal nutrition principles
about: Celebrates the autumn harvest — transitioning from summer's light meals to winter's warming comfort. Peak-season produce: pumpkin, squash, apples, pears, Brussels sprouts, mushrooms, cranberries, figs, nuts.
Autumn is nature's immune preparation peak: mushrooms (vitamin D, beta-glucans), apples (quercetin, fiber), pumpkin (beta-carotene), cruciferous vegetables (sulforaphane).
Transitions cooking methods: raw salads to roasting, sauteing, slow-cooking. Roasting caramelizes natural sugars making vegetables deeply satisfying without added sugar.
Warming spices become prominent: cinnamon (insulin sensitivity), ginger (anti-inflammatory), clove (highest antioxidant spice).
howItWorks: Pumpkin/squash as weekly staples: butternut, acorn, delicata — in soups, roasted, or sides
Apples and pears daily (peak season = maximum nutrition). Eat whole with skin for fiber
Mushrooms 3-4x/week: shiitake, cremini, portobello, chanterelle. Natural vitamin D source
Transition cooking: shift from raw salads to roasted, sauteed, slow-cooked dishes
Warming spices daily: cinnamon in oatmeal, ginger in stir-fries, nutmeg in soups
Begin immune prep: vitamin C (Brussels sprouts, peppers), zinc (pumpkin seeds, meat), vitamin D (mushrooms, fish)
Daily Tracker Checklist (6 items)
Seasonal autumn produce used (squash, apples, mushrooms)
Mushrooms consumed (3-4x/week target)
Apple or pear eaten today (whole, with skin)
Warming spices in at least one meal
Home-cooked roasted or slow-cooked meal
Vitamin C source consumed (Brussels sprouts, peppers, citrus)
avoidList: Out-of-season imported berries (low quality in autumn — use frozen)
Excessive pumpkin spice lattes (300-500 kcal sugar — not real pumpkin)
Halloween candy binges (enjoy mindfully, not the whole bag)
Heavy cream-based 'autumn' recipes (save for occasional treats)
Ignoring seasonal transition (stop forcing cold salads when body craves warm food)
Processed apple products: juice, sauce with sugar — eat whole apples
Scientific Sources & References
USDA Seasonal Produce Guide — Autumn
Cardwell G et al. Mushrooms as Vitamin D Source. Nutrients 2018
Khan N et al. Cinnamon: Multifaceted Medicinal Plant. ECAM 2014
European harvest tradition and seasonal eating principles
